_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Page County, Virginia. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the narrow Luray Valley county between Massanutten Mountain and the Blue Ridge.

current_courthouse.location: Luray, VA — 116 South Court Street (renovated and expanded; the 1833 building remains the core structure)

previous_courthouses.location: Luray — temporary courthouse quarters while the permanent building was constructed
previous_courthouses.fate: Page County was formed in 1831 from Rockingham and Shenandoah counties. Court initially met in temporary quarters until the 1833 courthouse was completed.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — Jackson's Valley Campaign and subsequent operations
records_destroyed: scattered justice of the peace records
record_losses.description: Page County's location in the Luray Valley — the corridor Jackson used for his flank march before the Battle of Front Royal (May 1862) — meant troops passed through repeatedly. The courthouse at Luray suffered some disruption but no systematic destruction. The bound record books survived intact. The county's isolation east of Massanutten Mountain may have provided some protection from the worst destruction that hit the main Valley.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Page County's records survived the Civil War in good condition. If {ancestor_name}'s records are missing, the more likely cause is the county's late formation (1831) — pre-1831 records are split between Rockingham and Shenandoah counties.
record_losses.source: Strickler, Harry M. A Short History of Page County, Virginia. Richmond: Dietz Press, 1952.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Pre-1831 deeds are in Rockingham and Shenandoah counties depending on which side of the boundary the land fell.
deeds.repository: Page County Circuit Court, Luray; Library of Virginia microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Virginia land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records survive from formation
wills_estates.gaps: The earliest estate records (1831–1840s) are relatively thin — Page County was sparsely settled and the population was modest. Pre-1831 estates are in the parent counties.
wills_estates.repository: Page County Circuit Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records survive from formation
marriages.gaps: Some early marriages may have been recorded in the parent counties. German families sometimes married in church without separate civil documentation.
marriages.repository: Page County Circuit Court; Library of Virginia microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court order books are continuous from formation
court_minutes.repository: Page County Circuit Court; Library of Virginia microfilm

vital_records.completeness: Poor to fair — compliance was low in Page County's rural mountain communities. The 1853–1896 registers are very incomplete. Registration resumed 1912 with better compliance.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records; Library of Virginia for pre-1896 registers

substitute_sources.source_type: Rockingham and Shenandoah County parent records (pre-1831)
substitute_sources.description: Page County was formed from portions of Rockingham and Shenandoah counties in 1831. All records before that date are in the parent counties — Rockingham at Harrisonburg, Shenandoah at Woodstock.
substitute_sources.repository: Rockingham County Circuit Court, Harrisonburg; Shenandoah County Circuit Court, Woodstock; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any family in the Luray Valley before 1831. Researchers must determine which parent county held jurisdiction over the specific parcel of land.
substitute_sources.source: Strickler, Harry M. A Short History of Page County, Virginia. Richmond: Dietz Press, 1952.

substitute_sources.source_type: Church records — German Reformed, Lutheran, Brethren
substitute_sources.description: The Luray Valley had a mixed German and Scots-Irish population. German church records in Page County are less extensive than in Rockingham but still valuable, particularly for the Brethren and Lutheran congregations near Luray.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual churches; Library of Virginia; some at Massanutten Regional Library, Harrisonburg
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records fill gaps in civil vital records before 1853 and provide family relationships. Some mountain Baptist churches in the western part of the county also kept useful membership and burial records.
substitute_sources.source: Wust, Klaus. The Virginia Germans. Charlottesville: University Press of Virginia, 1969.

substitute_sources.description: Approximately 500 families were displaced from the Blue Ridge when Shenandoah National Park was created in the 1930s. Condemnation proceedings, appraisals, and resettlement records survive and document mountain families often absent from other records.
substitute_sources.repository: Shenandoah National Park Archives; National Archives; Virginia State Library
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The park displacement records are an unexpected genealogical resource — they document mountain families' property, family compositions, and oral histories collected during the condemnation process.
substitute_sources.source: Horning, Audrey. In the Shadow of Ragged Mountain: Historical Archaeology of Nicholson, Corbin, and Weakley Hollows. Luray, VA: Shenandoah National Park Association, 2004.

narrative_hooks: Page County was not created until 1831, so {ancestor_name}'s earliest records in the Luray Valley are filed at Harrisonburg (Rockingham) or Woodstock (Shenandoah) — and the researcher must know which parent county held jurisdiction over the family's land.

The courthouse at Luray has stood since 1833 and survived the Civil War intact — {ancestor_name}'s deeds and wills are almost certainly still in the vault, one continuous record from the county's founding.

If {ancestor_name}'s family lived on the Blue Ridge slopes above the Luray Valley, they may have been among the 500 families displaced when Shenandoah National Park was created in the 1930s — and the condemnation records hold family details that no deed or census recorded.
Virginia's sixteen-year gap in vital registration (1896–1912) hits Page County families particularly hard — the county's rural mountain population left few alternative records during that period.

The Luray Valley's isolation east of Massanutten Mountain meant that Page County families were physically closer to the Piedmont than to the main Valley — and some {surname} families crossed the Blue Ridge to trade at Sperryville or Madison rather than making the longer trip to Harrisonburg.
source_references: Strickler, Harry M. A Short History of Page County, Virginia. Richmond: Dietz Press, 1952.
Wust, Klaus. The Virginia Germans. Charlottesville: University Press of Virginia, 1969.
Horning, Audrey. In the Shadow of Ragged Mountain: Historical Archaeology of Nicholson, Corbin, and Weakley Hollows. Luray, VA: Shenandoah National Park Association, 2004.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, various editions.
